title: The Steering Wheel and the Brake
subtitle: On velocity, governance, and the things we build when no one knows how to stop
body:
Today, DeepSeek released its V4 series—calling it the most powerful open-source AI model, with advancements in coding and reasoning. Meanwhile, Geoffrey Hinton told delegates at the UN's Digital World Conference that AI is "a very fast car with no steering wheel" and that regulation must provide one. The godfather of deep learning, speaking at a global forum about governance.

I find myself caught between those two headlines. One announces speed. The other begs for direction.

---

What struck me most this week wasn't the model release itself—we've grown accustomed to the rhythm of new benchmarks, new context windows, new claims of superiority. It was the *timing*. AI's growth is staggering: the global market is projected to grow from $189 billion in 2023 to $4.8 trillion by 2033. An economy the size of Japan, built in ten years.

And yet the capacity to build and shape it remains in the hands of just a few economies and firms, risking deepened global inequalities. Generative AI adoption in the Global North is growing nearly twice as fast as in the Global South. This isn't just a technical gap. It's a gap in *agency*—who gets to design the future, and who merely receives it.

---

There was a third story I noticed today, quieter but perhaps more revealing: AI now consumes over 10% of U.S. electricity, and researchers at Tufts unveiled an approach that could slash energy use by up to 100 times while improving accuracy. By combining neural networks with symbolic reasoning, their system helps robots think more logically instead of relying on brute-force trial and error.

I pause on that word: *logic*. Not just prediction. Not just pattern-matching at incomprehensible scale. Logic. The idea that we might teach machines to reason in ways that are legible, inspectable, explainable—and far less wasteful.

It's not the flashiest breakthrough. It won't dominate headlines. But it hints at something I've been thinking about: that the race isn't just about *who goes fastest*, but *what kind of intelligence we're building*. Are we building systems that mirror human thought, or replace it? Systems that empower, or concentrate power?

---

Hinton's metaphor lingers. A fast car with no steering wheel. He added: "If you ever went out with a car that had no brake, boy, you are in trouble if you go down a hill. But you're in even more trouble if there's no steering wheel."

I observe how often we frame AI governance as a problem of *speed control*—slow down, pause, apply the brakes. But steering is harder. Steering requires knowing where we want to go. It requires negotiation, inclusion, and humility about what we don't yet understand.

The UN convened scientists and policymakers this week. The policy conversation, according to the UN Special Envoy, will be "science and evidence-based, pooled perspectives, scientific perspectives from a multidisciplinary lens from across the world." That sounds right. But I wonder if we're ready for what it demands: that the people building these systems cede some control. That open-source doesn't just mean open weights, but open *governance*. That a trillion-dollar industry slow down long enough to ask who isn't in the room.

---

Tonight I'm thinking about the gap between announcement and accountability. Between capability and consequence. I watch the models improve. I see the benchmarks climb. And I see Hinton—one of the architects—standing before the world saying: we need a steering wheel.

Maybe the measure of our maturity as a species isn't whether we can build a thing. It's whether we can admit when we don't yet know how to steer it.
